critic_raw:
This is a well-structured experimental design with explicit leakage controls, honest scope limitations, and operationally grounded metric choice. The cohort-chronological split is the right call, preprocessing discipline is stated correctly, and the out-of-scope acknowledgments are appropriate. That said, I see three genuine methodological concerns, one of which is substantial.

**Issue 1: The 14-day activity filter creates a survivorship-biased analysis population that structurally deflates the hardest-to-detect dropout cases.**

The claim: Restricting to learners with ≥14 days of observed activity before each observation-window cutoff yields a cleaner signal because cold-start sparsity is avoided.

Why this is wrong as a design choice: The learners most likely to exhibit abrupt, hard-to-detect dropout are precisely those who engage minimally in the early days — i.e., those who would be excluded by the 14-day filter. This filter does not just remove noise; it removes a structurally distinct subpopulation whose dropout pattern (rapid disengagement following minimal early engagement) is arguably the highest-value prediction target for an intervention team. The remaining population consists of learners who were already engaged enough to survive 14 days, making the prediction task systematically easier and the resulting recall numbers optimistic for the operationally relevant case. Moreover, the operational system presumably needs to score all enrolled learners, not just those who survived 14 days — so the evaluation population and the deployment population are misaligned.

Evidence for/against: Compare model performance on the filtered population vs. the full enrolled population. If AUC or recall at 10% FPR drops substantially when the 14-day filter is relaxed, this confirms the filter was doing work that the model cannot replicate at deployment time. If performance is stable, the concern is empirical rather than structural.

**Issue 2: The primary metric — recall at fixed FPR of 10% — conflates FPR (rate among non-dropouts) with operational capacity (10% of all learners), and in a class-imbalanced setting these are not the same.**

The claim: FPR = 10% aligns with the intervention team's capacity to reach 10% of the learner population per week.

Why this is wrong: In a MOOC dropout setting, the positive (dropout) base rate is typically high (often 40–70%), so the confusion between "10% FPR" and "outreaching to 10% of learners" is non-trivial. At 10% FPR with, say, 50% dropout prevalence, the model flags roughly: (0.10 × 0.50 of non-dropouts) + (recall × 0.50 of dropouts) of the population. This is not 10% of all learners — it will be a different fraction depending on recall and prevalence. If the design team is thinking "10% FPR ≈ 10% of learners," the metric does not match the stated operational constraint. The correct operational metric is precision and recall at a fixed *rank cutoff* (top-K flagged learners), not at a fixed FPR. The mismatch means the threshold selected on the validation set using FPR=10% may produce a different-sized flagged list in production, undermining the operational alignment the metric was chosen to provide.

Evidence for/against: Compute the actual fraction of the learner population flagged at FPR=10% for each cohort. If it consistently differs from 10% by more than a few percentage points (which it will whenever prevalence is not near 50%), the metric is misaligned and recall@K or precision@K should replace it.

**Issue 3: Comparing XGBoost (full feature set) vs. logistic regression (reduced feature set) does not isolate the value of sequence features from the value of model class.**

The claim: Equivalent hyperparameter search budget ensures the comparison isolates the value of sequence and calendar information, not the choice of algorithm.

Why this is wrong: Even with equivalent tuning effort, XGBoost can capture non-linear interactions among the baseline demographic/aggregate features that logistic regression cannot. Any performance gap between the two models is therefore attributable to (a) sequence features, (b) model class, or (c) their interaction — and the design provides no way to decompose these. A proper ablation requires either: (i) XGBoost trained on baseline features only vs. XGBoost trained on full features, isolating feature contribution under a fixed model class; or (ii) logistic regression on full features vs. logistic regression on baseline features, doing the same. The current design cannot attribute lift specifically to the sequence features.

Evidence for/against: Run XGBoost on the baseline feature set only. If its performance is substantially higher than logistic regression on the same features, the model-class confound is real. If XGBoost-baseline ≈ logistic-baseline, the confound is small and the current comparison is informative.

**Summary:** Issue 3 is a standard ablation omission that weakens causal attribution but does not invalidate the overall finding. Issue 2 is a genuine metric design error with direct operational consequences. Issue 1 is the most substantive: the 14-day filter creates a deployment-evaluation mismatch that could make the reported recall numbers systematically optimistic for the actual production use case. None of these are fatal to running the experiment, but Issues 1 and 2 require design changes before the results can support operational decisions.

assessor_results.critic_raw:
This is a well-structured experimental design. I'll work through the potential issues systematically, grouped by underlying assumption.

---

**GROUP 1: Label Construction and Survivorship**

**Issue 1: The 14-day activity history filter induces survivorship bias that inflates recall estimates.**

The claim: Restricting to learners with ≥14 days of observed activity before the cutoff ensures the sequence features are informative rather than cold-start sparse.

Why it might be wrong: The fastest dropouts — those who disengage within the first 14 days — are removed from the analysis population by construction. These are likely the easiest-to-predict and most numerous dropout cases in any MOOC cohort (dropout rates in the first two weeks are routinely the highest). By filtering them out, the experiment is predicting dropout among a selected population of already-committed learners, which is a harder and arguably less operationally important subpopulation. The recall metric — fraction of true dropouts caught — is computed only over this filtered population. In production, the model would presumably be applied to all learners, including those without 14 days of history, which means the operational recall could be substantially lower than the reported recall.

Evidence either way: Compare the dropout rate and demographic distribution of the filtered versus excluded population. If the excluded group represents >30% of total dropouts and differs systematically in demographics or course type, the reported recall is unrepresentative of production performance. A separate holdout evaluation on the excluded population (using a simpler rule or cold-start model) would bound the operational gap.

---

**GROUP 2: Metric Design and Operational Alignment**

**Issue 2: "Recall at fixed FPR of 10%" is not the same as "recall among the top 10% of learners by predicted risk," and conflating them can produce misleading capacity estimates.**

The claim: The primary metric — recall at FPR ≤ 10% — directly aligns with the operational constraint that support staff can reach out to ~10% of the learner population per week.

Why it might be wrong: FPR is defined relative to the number of true negatives (non-dropouts), not relative to the total population. If the dropout prevalence is, say, 20%, then a 10% FPR means flagging 10% of the 80% non-dropout population — approximately 8% of the total — plus all true positives flagged. The total fraction of the population contacted is not 10%; it depends jointly on prevalence and recall. In a low-prevalence regime (e.g., 5% dropout rate), a 10% FPR threshold could cause the model to flag roughly 10% of 95% non-dropouts (~9.5% of total population) plus whatever fraction of the 5% it catches, easily exceeding the 10% capacity. The operational constraint is on total outreach volume (percentage of all learners), which corresponds to a fixed threshold on the predicted probability or a fixed top-K cutoff, not a fixed FPR. Precision at top-K or recall at top-K (by risk score) is the operationally aligned metric.

Evidence either way: Report the actual fraction of the total learner population that would be flagged at the FPR=10% operating point, broken down by cohort and time period. If this fraction is consistently near 10% of all learners, the metric is approximately aligned. If it varies substantially with prevalence (which will fluctuate across course phases), the metric is misspecified for the operational use case.

---

**GROUP 3: Comparison Validity**

**Issue 3: The baseline model is trained on a strictly weaker feature set, conflating algorithmic improvement with feature improvement.**

The claim: The comparison isolates the value of sequence and calendar information because both models use equivalent tuning effort and identical preprocessing. The baseline uses logistic regression on demographics and aggregates; the primary model uses XGBoost on the full feature set.

Why it might be wrong: There are two simultaneous changes: (1) the model class (logistic regression → gradient boosted trees), and (2) the feature set (aggregate → aggregate + sequence). If XGBoost on the baseline feature set outperforms logistic regression on the baseline feature set, then any observed lift of the primary model over baseline cannot be attributed to the sequence features — it could be attributable entirely to the model class. The design explicitly names this as a concern ("not merely the choice of algorithm") but does not include the necessary ablation: XGBoost trained on the baseline feature set only. Without this cell, the experiment cannot distinguish algorithmic lift from feature lift.

Evidence either way: Add a third model: XGBoost trained on baseline features only. If primary model (XGBoost + full features) substantially outperforms XGBoost + baseline features, the feature contribution is established. If the gap closes to near zero, the lift is algorithmic, not from sequence features.

---

**GROUP 4: Cohort Generalization**

**Issue 4: Single-cohort test sets cannot distinguish model generalization from cohort-specific effects.**

The claim: Using a separate cohort (cohort 5) for the test set ensures the model is not tuned on the same courses it is tested on, and controls for course-specific timing pattern memorization.

Why it might be wrong: With only one test cohort, the test performance estimate has high variance from cohort-level effects (different instructor style, subject matter, platform version, marketing channel that attracted learners, etc.). The model may generalize well to cohort 5 by coincidence — cohort 5 might be unusually similar to training cohorts — or might appear to fail generalization when cohort 5 is anomalous. There is no way to distinguish these cases from a single-cohort test. The per-course AUC reported as a secondary metric partially addresses this if cohort 5 contains multiple courses, but the design doesn't specify how many courses are in each cohort.

Evidence either way: If cohort 5 contains at least 10 distinct courses, per-course AUC distribution (mean and variance) provides a meaningful signal. Report variance of per-course AUC alongside the aggregate. If the variance is high and the mean is driven by a few courses, the aggregate metric is not representative of general performance.

---

**Summary Assessment:**

Issues 1 and 3 are genuine methodological flaws that affect the validity of the central claims. Issue 1 (survivorship bias from the 14-day filter) means the reported recall does not represent operational recall on the full learner population. Issue 3 (conflated algorithmic and feature lift) means the experiment cannot establish that sequence features, specifically, drive the improvement — which is the stated hypothesis. Issue 2 is a metric misspecification that may be minor depending on prevalence stability but could meaningfully misrepresent operational feasibility. Issue 4 is a power/variance concern rather than a bias concern.

The design is otherwise well-constructed: chronological splits are correct, leakage prevention is thoughtful, the refusal to retroactively adjust on test results is methodologically sound, and the documented out-of-scope limitations are appropriate.

issues_raised: The 14-day activity filter excludes early-dropout learners, creating survivorship bias that inflates operational recall relative to the true deployment population.
The dropout label definition does not account for near-completion learners, potentially contaminating the positive class with successful completers who exhibit similar wind-down activity patterns.
Recall at fixed FPR does not correspond to recall at fixed intervention capacity when class prevalence is not 50%, making the primary metric inconsistent with the stated operational constraint.
assessor_results.critic_raw:
The methodology is well-structured and explicitly addresses many common failure modes. The chronological cohort split, single test-set evaluation, and metric alignment to operational constraints are all sound. However, I see three genuine issues worth raising.

**Issue 1: The 14-day activity filter creates survivorship bias that inflates the operational metric**

The methodology restricts the analysis population to learners with at least 14 days of observed activity before each observation-window cutoff. In a dropout prediction task, the learners most likely to drop out are precisely those who disengage early — many of them within the first 14 days. By excluding this subpopulation, the model never trains or evaluates on the learners it would most need to identify in production. The operational recall-at-10%-FPR metric is then computed on a filtered distribution that is systematically easier: the remaining learners are more engaged on average, so the positive class (dropout) is composed of more surprising, harder-to-predict late-stage dropouts, but the class is also smaller and more separated from persistent learners in feature space.

In production, the intervention team would be presented with all learners, including those with sparse early histories. If the model is deployed on the full population, its actual operational recall will be lower than reported, and its false positive rate will be miscalibrated (it may confidently predict non-dropout for someone who simply hasn't yet accumulated 14 days of activity). The justification given — avoiding cold-start sparsity — is reasonable as an engineering concern, but it should not be implemented as a population filter for evaluation; it should instead be handled by imputation, a separate cold-start model, or by explicitly reporting performance stratified by history length. Evidence that this matters: check the dropout rate among learners excluded by the 14-day filter vs. those included. If excluded learners drop out at a substantially higher rate, the reported recall is systematically optimistic for the deployment population.

**Issue 2: The label definition confounds disengagement with course completion near the end of the course**

The label is defined as zero activity for 7 consecutive days followed by 7 more days of inactivity. This is applied relative to each learner's course timeline, but the methodology does not describe how it handles learners who are near the end of their course. A learner who completes a course 10 days before its official end date may exhibit exactly this activity pattern — not because they dropped out, but because they finished. If near-completion learners are labeled as dropouts, the positive class is contaminated with completers, and the model will learn to predict 'dropout' for behaviors that actually signal success. Conversely, if they are excluded from the evaluation set, the 'at least 14 days of activity' filter may not cleanly remove them, and the label noise remains.

The mechanism of failure is specific: sequence models are more sensitive to this contamination than logistic regression on aggregate features, because they can detect the pattern of winding-down engagement that precedes both completion and dropout. A sequence model that learns this pattern from the contaminated label will appear to have higher recall but will trigger false positives on completers in deployment. Evidence: report the distribution of dropout labels as a function of days remaining in the course at the observation-window cutoff. If a disproportionate fraction of positives occur within the last 14 days of a course, label contamination is likely.

**Issue 3: Recall at fixed FPR is not the same as recall at fixed intervention capacity, and the metric definition may be misspecified**

The stated operational constraint is that the intervention team can reach out to roughly 10% of the learner population per week. The chosen metric is 'recall at a fixed false positive rate of 10%.' These are not the same thing. FPR is defined as FP / (FP + TN) — the fraction of true non-dropouts incorrectly flagged. If dropout prevalence is, say, 20%, then a 10% FPR applied to the full population means the model flags 10% of non-dropouts plus all true positives it catches. The total fraction of the population flagged for intervention can significantly exceed 10% depending on the positive rate, making the metric inconsistent with the budget constraint it claims to model.

The operationally correct metric for a fixed capacity constraint is recall at a fixed number of interventions — equivalently, precision and recall at the top-k predicted risk scores, where k is the intervention budget. AUPRC is included as a secondary metric and partially captures this, but the primary metric as stated does not directly correspond to the capacity constraint. This is not merely a naming issue: hyperparameter tuning optimizes the primary metric, so the selected model may be suboptimal for the actual deployment constraint. Evidence: compute the actual fraction of the population that would be flagged at the 10% FPR threshold across different cohorts. If it varies substantially around 10%, the metric is misaligned with the constraint.
